summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a Jane Brody New York Times article about exercise and diets, pointing out its logical contradictions. She argues that Brody acknowledges diets don't work, yet still recommends them, and titles her piece to imply exercise causes weight loss despite evidence to the contrary. O'Neil sees this as a frustrating example of incoherent health advice that shames people for accepting their bodies.
key_points: The article being critiqued admits diets don't work but recommends them anyway
People accepting their bodies as they are is framed negatively by Brody
The title misleadingly implies exercise leads to weight loss when evidence suggests otherwise
Health journalism often contains internal contradictions that go unexamined
money_quotes: I'm off of Twitter and Facebook, so my blog is starting to become my go-to rant spot once again.
People are beginning to understand this.
They're starting to accept their bodies as they are and go on with their lives.
This makes Jane Brody feel bad for them, because they really should feel bad for themselves.
key_insight: Health advice that acknowledges its own ineffectiveness while still prescribing the same solutions reveals more about cultural attitudes toward bodies than about actual health.
